designer: Michael Westmore, supv. · sculpt by Vincent Niebla
knownFor: A working painter first — two dozen of his real canvases hang as set dressing in Kira's and Bashir's quarters.
reveal: Never spoke a single line in seven years, and still got an entire episode built around him. In “Who Mourns for Morn?” Shepherd appears on camera unmasked, as the patron asked to take Morn's empty barstool. By his own count of the fan mail, ~15 million people loved a character with no dialogue.

knownFor: Hollywood's resident heavy for thirty years — Total Recall, Tango & Cash, and villains on Kojak, Knight Rider, The A-Team.
reveal: The entire Cardassian species was reverse-engineered from his face. Westmore built the signature neck ridges directly off Alaimo's own prominent neck muscles — the man was nicknamed “The Neck.” So there is no clean before/after here: the makeup is a stylized Marc Alaimo.

portrait.author:
Alaimo,_Shimerman_and_Meany.jpg: Beth Madison
derivative work: Gyrobo (talk)

knownFor: Juilliard-trained stage actor; ex-husband of Voyager's Roxann Dawson; plays rhythm guitar in a band of Trek actors.
reveal: Hired for what was basically one line as a background Cardassian. The writers liked him so much they grew the part into the leader of the Cardassian Union. He didn't find out his character had a murdered wife and child until two episodes after it happened — and said he'd have played it sadder if he'd known.

knownFor: The franchise's great chameleon — also Brunt the Ferengi on DS9, and Commander Shran the Andorian on Enterprise.
reveal: Played a whole line of interchangeable Weyoun clones plus several unrelated species across Trek. Few actors have worn more designed faces in one universe without the audience clocking that it's the same man.

portrait.author: Super Festivals from Ft. Lauderdale, USA

knownFor: The Scorpio Killer in Dirty Harry (1971) — a face genre fans already feared.
reveal: His Cardassian makeup was kept light enough for the face to read, and he used every bit of it — playing the “plain, simple tailor” as a spy of flirtatious, unplaceable ambiguity. Robinson liked the character so much he later wrote a Garak novel himself.

knownFor: Clayton on Benson, the judge on Boston Legal, and the singing voice of Chef Louis in The Little Mermaid.
reveal: The smooth, half-finished face was the design thesis: a shapeshifter who can approximate a humanoid but never quite nails the details. The unfinished look is the character, not a limitation.

knownFor: The definitive creature performer of modern film — Abe Sapien in Hellboy, the Amphibian Man in The Shape of Water.
reveal: Played two utterly different creatures in the same film. Guillermo del Toro casts him precisely because the design erases the man completely — you never think “that's a guy in a suit,” which is the highest compliment the craft has.

knownFor: Caesar across the Planet of the Apes trilogy; Supreme Leader Snoke; King Kong.
reveal: The case that forced the industry to treat motion capture as acting rather than animation. The “design” here is a digital skin painted over a real, fully-performed take — a mask made of pixels instead of foam latex.

knownFor: A 7'2" Nigerian graphic-design student, reportedly discovered in a London pub. This was his only film role.
reveal: Cast almost entirely for his frame — the thing that could wear Giger's biomechanical suit and still move like it was alive. He studied mime and tai chi to get the walk. The purest possible answer to “who was under there?”: someone who was never seen again.

designer: Costume by John Mollo, from Ralph McQuarrie's concept
knownFor: Prowse: champion bodybuilder and Britain's beloved Green Cross Code road-safety man. Jones: the voice, uncredited on release.
reveal: A single designed face split across two performers who were never the same person — the body on set, the voice in post. The most recognizable mask in film history hides not one actor but the seam between two.

knownFor: The one-armed man, Frederick Sykes, that Richard Kimble chases through The Fugitive (1993).
reveal: A classically trained stage actor who gave a spotted Narn warlord real Shakespearean gravity — G'Kar's arc from schemer to spiritual leader is one of television's great redemptions, and none of Katsulas's own face survives the prosthetic.

knownFor: Danielle Rousseau, the shipwrecked French scientist on Lost.
reveal: A Croatian stage star who left the former Yugoslavia during the war and rebuilt her career in English under a Minbari bone crest. The headpiece and brow ridges reset the entire shape of her skull.

knownFor: Crom in Tron (1982); Sid the Snitch on Hill Street Blues.
reveal: The rare case where the design is worn in the hair: a Centauri's status is measured by the span of his peacock fan, so the higher Londo rises the more absurd and magnificent his silhouette. The tragedy underneath is all Jurasik.

knownFor: One of the most famous child actors alive — Will Robinson on Lost in Space, and the boy who wishes people into the cornfield in The Twilight Zone's “It's a Good Life.”
reveal: Grew up on camera, then disappeared entirely under Minbari makeup as the devoted acolyte Lennier. Also a working musician — half of the novelty duo behind “Fish Heads.”

knownFor: A big, warm Australian comic actor — the last thing you'd guess sits under the tentacled Luxan warrior.
reveal: Hours in the Henson Creature Shop chair turned a genial Aussie into Farscape's snarling, honor-bound D'Argo, facial tentacles and all. The design leaves nothing of the man showing.

knownFor: The Warrior Woman in Mad Max 2: The Road Warrior (1981), and a Bond girl in The Living Daylights.
reveal: Blue-skinned, bald, and serene as the plant priestess Zhaan — a full-body makeup so punishing it reportedly harmed her health and factored into her leaving the show. The most tranquil face on the ship cost the most to wear.

knownFor: Turns up in Star Wars: Revenge of the Sith (2005) as a young Grand Moff Tarkin.
reveal: The leather-clad, skull-faced Scorpius is one of sci-fi TV's great villains, with zero Wayne Pygram visible in him. Two of the genre's most memorably cold, calculating faces — both his, neither recognizable as the other.

knownFor: Played Worf across more Star Trek than any actor has played any single character — TNG, DS9, and four films.
reveal: The ridged forehead and mane rebuild the whole crown of his skull. Dorn even played his own grandfather, Colonel Worf, defending Kirk in Star Trek VI — same face, same makeup, a century apart.

portrait.author: Gage Skidmore from Peoria, AZ, United States of America

knownFor: Principal Snyder, the sardonic administrator eaten by a giant snake on Buffy the Vampire Slayer.
reveal: Under the enormous Ferengi ears and cranium is a wry Shakespearean actor who all but defined what a Ferengi is — after helping debut the species, to a rough reception, in TNG's first season. He made the design stick by acting the hell out of it.

knownFor: A one-man android dynasty — he also played Data's brother Lore, their creator Dr. Noonien Soong, and decades later Altan Soong on Picard.
reveal: Gold-white skin, yellow contact lenses, and a stillness that reads as not-quite-human. Subtler than a Klingon forehead, but doing the same job — erasing the ordinary man to leave something uncanny.

knownFor: A veteran comic actor — and, like Odo's René Auberjonois, an alum of the sitcom Benson.
reveal: The mottled, spotted Talaxian head was one of Voyager's most involved daily makeups. Two Benson castmates ended up unrecognizable on two different Star Treks — proof the era's makeup chair could swallow anyone.

designer: Burn makeup by David B. Miller / Kevin Yagher
knownFor: The year before Freddy, he was Willie — the gentle, sympathetic alien — in the sci-fi miniseries V.
reveal: The burn makeup buries a working character actor so completely that a generation knows only the scars and the glove. He went from beloved friendly alien to the most-feared face in horror in about twelve months.

portrait.author: No machine-readable author provided. Västgöten assumed (based on copyright claims).

designer: Bob Keen, from Clive Barker's design
knownFor: A Liverpool stage actor and lifelong friend of author Clive Barker, going back to their teenage theatre company.
reveal: The precise grid of pins is driven into a face otherwise erased in chalk-white prosthetic. Bradley nearly took a different role; the Cenobite makeup made the part — and hid the man — permanently.

knownFor: The definitive Jason — a career stuntman who wore the hockey mask across four films.
reveal: A stuntman who survived catastrophic burns early in his career and turned physical performance into a craft. He later created and played his own monster, Victor Crowley, in Hatchet. The mask is the point: it makes the man a blank the audience fills with dread.

designer: Cast for his own frame; designs vary by film
knownFor: The go-to creature performer of modern horror — Mama, the Crooked Man in The Conjuring 2, the leper in REC, and more.
reveal: Botet has Marfan syndrome, giving him an extraordinarily tall, elongated, hyper-flexible frame — so studios cast him exactly the way Alien cast Bolaji Badejo: for the body that can wear the design and move wrong. He keeps a full-body cast on file for effects work.

knownFor: A career built on disappearing — his debut was the prehistoric Amoukar in Quest for Fire (1981), performed entirely in makeup and an invented language, and he was Vincent under the lion's mane in TV's Beauty and the Beast.
reveal: Filed horns, red skin, and a slab jaw turn Perlman into Hellboy, but vanishing is his whole métier — few actors have logged more screen-hours unrecognizable and still built a face audiences love.

designer: IT (2017) prosthetic team, over Skarsgård's own features
knownFor: The youngest of the acting Skarsgårds — son of Stellan, brother of Alexander and Gustaf.
reveal: The Victorian clown makeup and buck teeth do a lot, but the truly unsettling parts — the drifting, misaligned eyes and the too-wide smile — Skarsgård produced himself, live on the day, without effects. The design and the performer meet in the middle of the uncanny.

portrait.author: Gage Skidmore from Peoria, AZ, United States of America

knownFor: A character actor whose most famous feature he brought himself: the eyes.
reveal: The bulging, manic stare of the Klingon chancellor was no prosthetic — O'Reilly could pop his own eyes wide, and the directors just kept pushing him to do it more.

knownFor: A busy Trek utility player — also a Vulcan captain, a Changeling, and a Hur'q, all unrecognizable as each other.
reveal: The one-eyed Klingon general is buried under a full ridged appliance and beard; Hertzler is a lean, clean-shaven stage actor underneath.

knownFor: Played several different Ferengi across TNG and DS9 before landing Quark's hapless brother.
reveal: The oversized ears and cranium erase him completely — the gentle, nervous Rom is a warm comic invention wearing a lot of latex.

portrait.author: The original uploader was Hypertrek at English Wikipedia.

knownFor: The first Ferengi to join Starfleet — a full character arc played entirely under prosthetics.
reveal: A congenital kidney condition kept Eisenberg small his whole life, so a man in his late twenties convincingly played a Ferengi teenager. He was beloved by fans until his death in 2019.

knownFor: A distinguished stage and film actress leading the enemy of the entire Dominion War.
reveal: The smooth, unfinished Changeling face — deliberately not-quite-human — hides a warm, expressive performer behind cold, gelid features.

knownFor: Won an Oscar as Nurse Ratched in One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest.
reveal: Here the design is the elaborate Bajoran headdress and ear-cuff more than the face — but the smiling, poisonous piety is a masterclass in playing the villain everyone underestimates.

knownFor: Went on to become an in-demand prestige-TV director (The Americans, Star Trek, House of Cards).
reveal: The half-Klingon forehead ridges reset her whole brow. She was also once married to Casey Biggs — two people who spent the nineties unrecognizable on the same lot.

knownFor: A South African actress who made the hive's face a thing of eerie seduction.
reveal: Pallid grey skin, implants, and a detachable head-and-spine turn Krige into the Collective made flesh; she returned to play the Queen again in Voyager's finale.

knownFor: The very first Klingon ever seen on screen — in the original series, back in 1967.
reveal: Colicos originated the Klingon as a smooth-faced schemer in the sixties, then returned decades later, now under the full ridged makeup the species had grown, closing a thirty-year loop.

knownFor: The reluctant ex-Borg who became Voyager's breakout, and returned decades later on Picard.
reveal: Lighter than most on this wall — the ocular implant and facial appliances are the design — but the silver Borg hardware still does the job of turning a person into salvaged tech.

knownFor: Flounder in Animal House, and Dr. Axelrod on St. Elsewhere.
reveal: Londo's anxious aide is a mostly-hair Centauri transformation, but the sweetness Furst brought — and one legendary little finger-wave to a severed head — made Vir a fan favorite.

knownFor: G'Kar's fierce Narn attaché, under a full head-and-face prosthetic.
reveal: A living proof of the thesis: the role was later played by a different actress under the identical makeup, and many viewers never noticed — the design is that total.

knownFor: A one-scene comic alien who became one of the show's most quoted cult figures.
reveal: Buried under a lumpy, snaggle-toothed prosthetic and speaking in his own broken grammar, Zathras is unrecognizable as the working American actor Choate was underneath.

knownFor: The grey-skinned Nebari thief who became the heart of the crew.
reveal: Monochrome makeup, black eyes, and a jittering physicality turn an Australian actress into something skittish and alien; the design and the movement do it together.

knownFor: The half-masked Stykera who ferries the dying into the next world.
reveal: Half his face is sealed behind a mask that glows when his true nature shows — a design built so that you never quite see the whole man, which is the point.

knownFor: An Icelandic-born poet and writer — a gentle giant entirely at odds with the role.
reveal: Behind the stitched mask of human skin was a soft-spoken literary man who found the shoot miserable and the character haunting. The mask is the whole performance.

knownFor: Went on to direct films himself — including The Last Starfighter and The Boy Who Could Fly.
reveal: The blank white mask is a Captain Kirk mask — a William Shatner likeness spray-painted pale and reshaped. The most featureless face in horror started as a Star Trek toy.

knownFor: Born William Henry Pratt — a soft-spoken, courtly Englishman.
reveal: Jack Pierce's flat-topped skull, heavy brow, and neck bolts took hours to build and defined the monster forever. The gentle Pratt vanished so completely the makeup became the character's face in the public mind.

knownFor: “The Man of a Thousand Faces” — the patron saint of this entire wall.
reveal: Chaney designed and applied his own agonizing makeup, reportedly using wire and paint to wrench his features into the skull-faced Phantom. He invented the craft everyone here practices.

knownFor: Son of the Man of a Thousand Faces, carrying the family trade into the sound era.
reveal: Jack Pierce applied yak hair in layers, shooting the transformation frame by frame as more was added. A whole dynasty of vanishing, father to son.

knownFor: Over seven feet tall — and, the very same year, the Bigfoot in Harry and the Hendersons.
reveal: In one year Hall wore two of the era's great creature suits: the murderous alien hunter and the gentlest Sasquatch in movie history. Almost nobody realized it was the same towering man.

knownFor: Dr. Frank-N-Furter in The Rocky Horror Picture Show, and later Pennywise on TV.
reveal: Rob Bottin's towering red demon — sweeping horns, cloven everything — swallows Curry whole. Hours in the chair to make one of cinema's most physically imposing devils out of a nimble character actor.

knownFor: A child actress at the center of the most notorious horror film ever made.
reveal: Dick Smith's landmark cracked-and-cratered possession makeup transformed a twelve-year-old into a demon — with the voice dubbed by Mercedes McCambridge. Two performers, one face.

knownFor: One of cinema's most recognizable faces — progressively erased across a single film.
reveal: Chris Walas won an Oscar for the staged decay that turns Goldblum from man to insect. The horror is watching a familiar face disappear a layer at a time.

knownFor: The rat-fanged silent-film vampire whose look still defines the archetype.
reveal: So total was Schreck's transformation that a whole later film, Shadow of the Vampire, was built on the joke that he must have been a real vampire. The makeup outlived the man's own face in memory.

portrait.author: Unknown photographer and publisher - reverse is blank apart from message and 1922 postmark

knownFor: One of the great chameleons — an actor with almost no fixed screen face.
reveal: Greg Cannom's Oscar-winning work runs Oldman through an ancient armored prince, a wizened old count, and a bat-thing — several different unrecognizable faces inside one performance.

knownFor: Went from this mossy, unhinged ghost straight into playing Batman for the same director.
reveal: Ve Neill's grimy, mildewed makeup plus wild improvisation turn Keaton unrecognizable — in only about seventeen minutes of total screen time.

knownFor: An effects artist who also wears his own creatures — the Xenomorph across three Alien films, and the title beast of Pumpkinhead.
reveal: The rare person who both builds the suit and vanishes into it. When you were terrified of the adult Alien in the sequels, you were often watching Woodruff perform it.

knownFor: One of the show's most acclaimed writers — a future Emmy winner for the series.
reveal: The writer who'd go on to script the show's funniest, smartest episodes first served it from inside a slimy full-body fluke-monster suit. Nobody watching “The Host” had any idea.

knownFor: Discovered largely for his height — over seven feet tall — while working as a hospital porter.
reveal: Under the yak-and-mohair Wookiee suit, Mayhew built one of film's most beloved characters with body language alone, never a visible inch of his own face or a word of dialogue.

knownFor: The only actor to appear in every film of the main Star Wars saga.
reveal: A trained mime, initially reluctant to take the part, sealed inside a rigid gold shell that cut and pinched — and he still made a fussy droid one of the most recognizable performances in the series.

knownFor: A cabaret and comedy performer, under three foot eight, folded inside the dome.
reveal: When R2 rocks, waddles, and tips, that's often Baker crammed into the shell working the body by hand — the most expressive silent character in the galaxy driven from inside.

knownFor: A martial-arts and stunt performer who made a career of masked roles — Toad, Snake Eyes, and more.
reveal: Red-and-black tattooed makeup and a crown of horns hide Park completely, and his voice was even dubbed by someone else — the menace is pure movement.

knownFor: Also lent his voice to Treebeard — two very different characters in one trilogy.
reveal: The dwarf beard, nose, and brow buried him so thoroughly that he was allergic to the prosthetics, which burned his skin — he paid for the transformation in real discomfort.

knownFor: A Māori actor who played several of the trilogy's monsters — the Uruk-hai Lurtz, the Witch-king, and Gothmog.
reveal: He is the face of Middle-earth's villainy multiple times over, and you'd never pick him out of a crowd — each design erases him into a different nightmare.

portrait.author: Super Festivals from Ft. Lauderdale, USA

knownFor: A rubber-faced comedian buried under someone else's rubber for once.
reveal: Rick Baker's green fur was so unbearable to wear that Carrey was reportedly coached by a CIA operative on enduring torture just to get through the daily hours in the chair.

portrait.author: The original uploader was Théo du 14 at French Wikipedia.

knownFor: A transformation so complete it won the film an Academy Award for makeup.
reveal: In full old-woman prosthetic, Williams reportedly wandered a San Francisco neighborhood in character and went entirely unrecognized — the truest test any of these faces can pass.

portrait.author: U.S. Air Force photo / Airman 1st Class Tabitha M. Mans

knownFor: A former child star who became the soul of the entire Apes saga.
reveal: John Chambers' ape makeup earned a special Academy Award and let McDowall act — subtly, movingly — through a fully sculpted chimpanzee face across five films.

knownFor: One of Britain's finest actors, delivering a landmark performance almost entirely unseen.
reveal: Christopher Tucker's makeup was so extensive that applying it took the better part of a day, and Hurt could only shoot on alternate days. A whole face, and a whole soul, built from the outside in.

knownFor: A model-turned-actor rendered as a scaled blue shapeshifter.
reveal: The look was hours of individually applied blue prosthetic scales over near-total body paint — one of the most punishing makeups in modern film, worn to play a character defined by having no fixed face at all.

knownFor: Frasier Crane, of all people, under blue fur and fangs.
reveal: Rick Baker turned the urbane sitcom voice of Frasier into the leonine mutant Hank McCoy — a casting joke that works precisely because the design hides him so well.

portrait.author: U.S. Navy photo by Lithographer 3rd Class Christen R. Nicholas

knownFor: A grotesque, sewer-dwelling reinvention of a campy comic villain.
reveal: Sallow skin, stringy hair, flipper hands, and black bile turn a familiar comedic actor into something genuinely repellent — one of the era's boldest star transformations.

knownFor: A beloved, instantly recognizable British actor — completely invisible in the role.
reveal: Nighy performed in a dotted grey capture suit; the writhing octopus beard and barnacled face were painted on digitally over his real, filmed performance. A landmark of the craft's newest form.

knownFor: Perhaps the most prolific franchise creature actor alive — an Ewok at eleven, then Willow, then two different Harry Potter roles.
reveal: A whole career spent inside designed faces: the Ewok Wicket, the professor Flitwick and the goblin Griphook, the Leprechaun, and more. Few performers have vanished into more separate looks.

knownFor: A leading dramatic actor rendered noseless and serpentine.
reveal: Prosthetics flattened his features and digital work erased his nose entirely, turning one of cinema's most handsome faces into a slit-nostrilled snake. The removal of a nose does most of the horror.

knownFor: An actor who built a whole second career under other skins — also the green-skinned Gamora.
reveal: Blue, ten feet tall, and entirely performance-captured, Neytiri is Saldaña's face and body remapped onto a Na'vi. Between this and Gamora, she may be the most-watched least-recognized star of her generation.

knownFor: Half a century inside two of the most famous characters on Earth — the towering yellow bird and the trash-can grouch, both at once.
reveal: From inside the eight-foot bird suit, one arm held aloft to work the head, Spinney read scripts taped inside Big Bird's chest via a tiny monitor. Two icons, one unseen man, from 1969 until he retired in 2018.

knownFor: A champion bodybuilder from Pumping Iron, deaf since childhood, who became the green giant of Friday-night syndication.
reveal: Bill Bixby was the man; Ferrigno was the monster — green body paint, a fright wig, and pure physical presence. No dialogue, just the transformation, for five seasons of endless reruns.

knownFor: A prolific little-person performer who lived inside beloved characters across decades of reruns — the hairball Cousin Itt, the robot Twiki, and even an Ewok.
reveal: You knew the “bidi-bidi-bidi” robot and the walking wig, but never the acrobat and former circus performer inside them. Silla built a whole career being unrecognizable on syndicated TV.

knownFor: Decades later, the cranky dad Frank Barone on Everybody Loves Raymond.
reveal: Under the flat skull, zipper scar, and neck bolts, Boyle plays the Monster for laughs and pathos both — putting on the ritz. Nothing of the future sitcom curmudgeon shows through.

designer: Prosthetic makeup, Masters of the Universe
knownFor: A revered stage actor — Broadway's Dracula, and Nixon in Frost/Nixon.
reveal: One of America's most serious theatrical actors took the blue-skulled cartoon villain because his kids loved He-Man, and called it a favorite role. Not a trace of the distinguished leading man survives the death's-head makeup.

production: Coming to America / The Nutty Professor
knownFor: Playing entire rooms of people by himself — old barbers, a lounge singer, and later the whole Klump family.
reveal: Rick Baker's makeup let Murphy vanish into half a dozen characters at once, including an elderly Jewish man arguing in a barbershop. Audiences watched whole conversations before realizing it was one actor.

knownFor: An Academy Award winner (An Officer and a Gentleman) rendered fully reptilian.
reveal: As the Drac warrior stranded alongside a human enemy, Gossett acts a tender, complex performance through a full scaled prosthetic head — an Oscar-winning face nobody would recognize.

knownFor: The lead Newcomer detective of a cult syndicated sci-fi show and its run of TV movies.
reveal: The bald, mottled, spotted Tenctonez skull was a daily transformation for Pierpoint, who carried a whole immigrant-allegory drama through hours of appliance work.

knownFor: Neidermeyer in Animal House, and the browbeating father in Twisted Sister's “We're Not Gonna Take It” video.
reveal: Buffy's first big bad is a bat-faced ancient vampire in full prosthetic — worlds away from the smug authority figures that made Metcalf a pop-culture staple.

knownFor: The rare comedian who plays the hero, the villain, and the monster in the same film.
reveal: Already Austin and Dr. Evil, Myers vanished a third time into the grotesque Scottish henchman under a mountain of prosthetic — three unrecognizable performances stacked in one franchise.

knownFor: The most recognizable action star of his era, encased in blue-and-silver ice.
reveal: Pale-blue skin, a glowing cryo-suit, and a dome of frost bury even Schwarzenegger's famous face — proof that the era's makeup chair could obscure the biggest star in the world.

knownFor: One of the first fully performance-captured lead characters in a live-action film — years before the technique won acclaim.
reveal: Best performed Jar Jar on set in a costume and headpiece, later replaced by CGI. He weathered a brutal backlash for a pioneering motion-capture performance, and Star Wars eventually welcomed him back on screen.

knownFor: The green-skinned, red-horned demon who ran a karaoke bar and read souls through song.
reveal: Hours of green makeup and horns each shooting day turned the warm, big-hearted Hallett into the show's most lovable demon. He died far too young, in 2009, at thirty-three.

knownFor: The intense character actor of Reservoir Dogs and Pulp Fiction, gone full chimpanzee general.
reveal: Rick Baker's ape makeup let Roth build a snarling, physical menace entirely through a sculpted primate face — a performance many rate above the film around it.

knownFor: The bald, intense lead of The Shield — a cop drama fixture turned rock monster.
reveal: A lifelong comics fan, Chiklis insisted on a practical foam-rubber rock suit rather than CGI, and spent hours a day being bolted into it. The gruff TV detective disappears completely into orange stone.

portrait.author:
Michaelchiklis.jpg: Albert Domasin from Los Angeles, united states
derivative work: Beao

knownFor: A career chameleon — Agent Smith, Elrond, V, and the voice of Megatron.
reveal: Prosthetics and digital work strip his face down to a crimson skull. Weaving has made a specialty of being unplaceable; here he's not even recognizably human.

knownFor: A WWE main-eventer who became one of the surprise comic actors of the MCU.
reveal: Hours of red-and-grey ritual scarring painted over every visible inch turned a giant wrestler into the deadpan, literal-minded Drax — the makeup as much a part of the joke as the delivery.

knownFor: The red-haired companion Amy Pond on Doctor Who — here, a blue-and-metal cyborg.
reveal: Gillan shaved her head and sat through hours of blue prosthetics and mechanical implants to become the half-machine Nebula, unrecognizable from her Doctor Who fame.

portrait.author: Gage Skidmore from Peoria, AZ, United States of America

knownFor: An Academy Award winner (12 Years a Slave) who chose to make her Star Wars debut entirely invisible.
reveal: The tiny, goggle-eyed, thousand-year-old pirate is Nyong'o rendered fully in performance capture — an Oscar-winning face nobody sees, by her own choice.

knownFor: The masked warlord of the Wasteland — and, remarkably, the villain of the very first Mad Max.
reveal: Keays-Byrne played the Toecutter in the 1979 original, then returned decades later for the same director as a different masked tyrant. Behind the breathing-mask and armor, one actor bookends the whole saga.

knownFor: One of the most recognizable leading men working — buried past all recognition.
reveal: Under Joel Harlow's reptilian villain makeup, there is no Idris Elba to find. It's among the most complete disappearing acts a bankable star has ever agreed to.

knownFor: Mr. Eko on Lost and Adebisi on Oz — a commanding presence, here scaled and fanged.
reveal: Full crocodilian prosthetics — ridged hide, jutting jaw, ruined skin — erase a striking actor into a swamp monster.

knownFor: The silent, blue-eyed face of the dead in the biggest fantasy series of the decade.
reveal: A career stunt performer under hours of icy prosthetic (Richard Brake originated the look earlier). The most feared figure in Westeros never speaks — the design and the stillness carry all of it.

knownFor: Matthew Crawley of Downton Abbey, transformed into a horned, fanged recluse.
reveal: Stevens performed on stilts in a muscle suit and grey capture pyjamas, with the horned Beast painted on digitally over his eyes and expression. The heartthrob is in there, driving all of it, unseen.

production: Dawn of the Planet of the Apes
knownFor: A landmark motion-capture villain, the scarred bonobo who turns on his own kind.
reveal: Kebbell performed Koba's every twitch and rage on set in a capture suit; Weta rendered the scarred ape over the top. Proof that the mo-cap craft had grown deep enough to build a great screen villain.

knownFor: A trained dancer who built a career on physical, transformed roles — later the mummy Ahmanet and Star Trek's Jaylah.
reveal: As the bladed henchwoman Gazelle she fights on prosthetic sword-legs, a design that turns a dancer's body into a weapon. A performer who keeps disappearing into other shapes.

knownFor: A gravelly leading man who anchored the biggest crossover in film history as a fully digital villain.
reveal: Brolin performed the Mad Titan in a capture rig; the purple, chin-cleft giant was built over his face and performance frame by frame. The century's most consequential movie villain is a man you never actually see.

knownFor: A ubiquitous character actor — the coroner Mike Spencer on True Blood, and heavies across 24, Prison Break, and The Orville.
reveal: The ridged brow, mottled skin, and unnervingly wide Denobulan grin bury a familiar TV face under a full appliance. Phlox's whole charm is a smile that isn't quite Billingsley's.

knownFor: The man of a thousand Trek aliens — the hunted Tosk, a Romulan, and a Jem'Hadar, all played by the same unrecognized face.
reveal: Under the full reptilian appliance of the Xindi war-leader is a prolific Trek journeyman who'd already vanished into four other species across the franchise. Fans know the roles and never the man.

knownFor: A working fashion model before she was the Vulcan science officer.
reveal: Lighter than most on this wall — the upswept brows and pointed ears are the design — but it's the standing marker that the 2000s were, in fact, a Star Trek decade after all.

knownFor: The most beloved bounty hunter in the galaxy — a whole cult built around a helmet that never comes off.
reveal: Bulloch built one of cinema's most obsessed-over characters with body language and a T-visor, never a visible feature. He even doubled as an Imperial officer elsewhere in the same film.

knownFor: Brienne of Tarth on Game of Thrones — here, a chrome-armored commander whose face never once appears.
reveal: A striking, instantly recognizable actor agreed to a role sealed entirely inside mirror-finish armor. You watch Phasma for two films and never see a single feature of the person inside.

knownFor: The strangest entry on the wall — a living actor wearing a dead one's face.
reveal: Henry performed Tarkin in a motion-capture rig so ILM could rebuild the late Peter Cushing's likeness over him, frame by frame. Cushing died in 1994; the designed face here is a resurrection.

knownFor: One of the most in-demand faces in Hollywood — starring in a role that hides it almost entirely.
reveal: The helmet stays on by creed, so the body in the beskar is frequently not even Pascal — stunt performers Brendan Wayne and Lateef Crowder wear the armor for much of the action, with Pascal's voice on top. A lead role split across three bodies.

character: The Wicked Witch of the West
knownFor: A former kindergarten teacher who became the most famous green face in movie history.
reveal: The ur-example of this whole wall — a makeup transformation that aired on television every year for generations. The copper-based green paint was toxic, and a trapdoor flame effect once left Hamilton with serious burns.

knownFor: The withered, half-mechanical creator of the Daleks — the face of the longest-running sci-fi franchise on Earth.
reveal: A single blue eye in the middle of a ruined skull, wired into a life-support chair; the actor's real face and body vanish into the machine. Multiple actors have worn the mask since Michael Wisher originated it in 1975.

knownFor: A serious indie actor and acting teacher — sealed inside a radio-controlled turtle.
reveal: The 1990 Turtles were Henson's Creature Shop animatronic suits with servo-driven faces; performers sweated inside while puppeteers worked the expressions. Pais was rare in doing both body and voice for Raphael himself.

designer: Peter Owen & the Burton makeup team
knownFor: A prolific creature performer who has spent forty years inside other shapes — later Keenser in the Star Trek reboot.
reveal: He played every single Oompa Loompa, replicated into an army of identical little men, performing the same routines over and over so they could be composited into a crowd of himself.

knownFor: The flamboyant Emcee of Cabaret and a fixture of stage and screen — rendered a blue, tattooed teleporter.
reveal: Indigo skin, a pointed tail, and rows of angelic sigils carved across his face took the better part of a day to apply. The result is unrecognizable as one of theatre's most recognizable personalities.

knownFor: An Oscar-winning writer-director who quietly performed the film's most lovable side character himself.
reveal: The gentle, soft-spoken rock gladiator is Waititi in a capture suit, directing the movie from inside one of its own creatures — a face made of stone over a face nobody realized was the guy in charge.

knownFor: A voice and a face everyone knows — poured entirely into a dragon.
reveal: Cumberbatch didn't just voice Smaug; he performed him in a capture suit, slithering across the floor to give the dragon its serpentine movement. He performed the Necromancer the same way in the same films.

portrait.author:
Benedict Cumberbatch at the London premiere of Tinker Tailor Soldier Spy. BFI South Bank, Tuesday 13th September 2011: Sam Hughes from UK
derivative work: RanZag (talk)

knownFor: “The Man of a Thousand Voices” — very nearly the entire Warner Bros. cast was one throat.
reveal: Bugs Bunny, Daffy Duck, Porky Pig, Tweety, Sylvester, and hundreds more were all Blanc. The gag: the man who was Bugs was, by his own account, allergic to carrots.

knownFor: The counterpart to Mel Blanc — the voice behind a generation of Saturday mornings.
reveal: Rocky the Flying Squirrel, the spy Natasha, Warner's Granny, and Cindy Lou Who in the original Grinch. Chuck Jones reportedly said she was not the female Mel Blanc — Mel Blanc was the male June Foray.

knownFor: By screen-credit count, among the most prolific performers in film history — and almost none of it his face.
reveal: He has voiced Fred Jones since 1969 and, for years now, Scooby-Doo himself — two characters in the same scene. He's also Megatron, and the go-to creator of nearly any animal sound you've heard on screen.

knownFor: Also one of comedy's great directors — Little Shop of Horrors, Dirty Rotten Scoundrels, Bowfinger.
reveal: The hand and voice inside Yoda, Miss Piggy, Fozzie Bear, Grover, and Cookie Monster. You know all of them intimately and would never pick him out of a room.

portrait.author: The Conmunity -  Pop Culture Geek from Los Angeles, CA, USA

knownFor: The steady, noble baritone that a whole generation trusts implicitly.
reveal: Cullen reportedly modeled Optimus's voice on his own brother, a Marine, who told him a real leader is strong enough to be gentle. He's also, improbably, the mournful voice of Eeyore.

knownFor: The most famous ten-year-old boy on television — voiced, for decades, by a grown woman.
reveal: Cartwright is also Nelson, Ralph Wiggum, and Todd Flanders. The definitive bratty schoolboy voice has, the whole time, been a woman in a booth.

character: Moe, Apu, Wiggum & Comic Book Guy
knownFor: A whole town's worth of citizens, all one man.
reveal: Azaria voices a huge share of Springfield — the bartender, the police chief, the comic-shop owner and dozens more — often trading lines with himself across a single scene.

knownFor: The best-known face in the galaxy — Luke Skywalker — attached to a laugh nobody connects to him.
reveal: For thirty years the definitive animated Joker has been Hamill. The reveal everyone does a double-take at: the wide-eyed farm boy of Star Wars is also the cackling clown prince of crime.

knownFor: For a whole generation, the one true Batman — a face they never saw.
reveal: Conroy voiced Batman across three decades and gave him two distinct voices, one for Bruce and one for the cowl. To millions he simply is Batman, entirely by sound.

portrait.author: Super Festivals from Ft. Lauderdale, USA

knownFor: Perhaps the most recognizable voice in the English language — attached to two characters whose faces are not his.
reveal: The voice of Mufasa and of Darth Vader (whose body on set was David Prowse). One instrument became two of cinema's most iconic figures, and you never saw him play either.

knownFor: A one-man cartoon repertory company across two eras of animation.
reveal: Both halves of Ren & Stimpy, then Fry and half of Futurama's cast, and later a returning Bugs and Elmer Fudd. A career of being a dozen people who don't share his face.

portrait.author: Ewen Roberts from San Diego, United States

knownFor: Very possibly the most-heard voice of the past twenty-five years of animation.
reveal: Bubbles, Timmy Turner, Raven, Twilight Sparkle, and a definitive Harley Quinn — a single performer behind a huge slice of the childhoods of two generations.

knownFor: The man who was Godzilla — inside the suit for a dozen films across eighteen years.
reveal: Nakajima studied animals at the zoo to build the monster's walk, then endured a suit so hot and heavy he'd pour sweat out of it by the cupful. The most destructive figure in film history was one determined stuntman, unseen and uncredited to most of the world.

knownFor: The silver-and-red giant at the root of an entire genre — worn by one man nobody could name.
reveal: Furuya was sealed inside the original Ultraman suit, the mask fixed in a single serene expression, so every ounce of the hero's presence had to come from posture and movement alone.

knownFor: The second great Godzilla — and, earlier, the smog-monster Hedorah.
reveal: Satsuma carried the monster through the entire Heisei run inside suits so punishing he reportedly passed out in them, refusing to break character. A whole era of Godzilla is one man's endurance.

knownFor: Godzilla for most of the Millennium films — and a veteran Super Sentai (Power Rangers) suit actor besides.
reveal: A career spent being the monster, then the giant robot, then the villain of the week — Kitagawa is a lifer of the suit, recognizable to fans only by the way his Godzilla moves.

knownFor: The American Godzilla — the suit replaced by a capture rig, the human performance kept.
reveal: Storm, a martial artist, performed Godzilla in motion capture for the Legendary films, carrying the suitmation tradition into pixels. Even digital, there's a specific person deciding how the king of the monsters moves.

knownFor: An erudite actor — with a doctorate in art history — encased in Rob Bottin's steel.
reveal: Only Weller's jaw shows below the visor, so he trained with a movement coach for weeks to build RoboCop's precise mechanical carriage. The character lives entirely in a mouth and a walk.

knownFor: A busy leading man — Bones, Éomer, Butcher — who agreed to keep his face hidden the entire film.
reveal: Honoring the comic's rule that Dredd never unmasks, Urban plays the whole role from the mouth down, under the helmet, for every minute of screen time. The scowl is the performance.

knownFor: The other man in the very first Godzilla suit — Nakajima's older, near-uncredited counterpart.
reveal: Tezuka shared the original 1954 suit and then played Anguirus, whose heavy carapace made it hard for him to even stand upright. History remembers one man in the first Godzilla suit; there were two.

knownFor: The suit actor who was two of Godzilla's most famous opponents.
reveal: Hirose was Toho's suitmation King Kong — peering out through a special close-up head — and then the three-headed golden King Ghidorah, working one head himself while wires and puppeteers handled the rest.

knownFor: Carried the King of the Monsters through the final classic-era film, then spent years in tokusatsu suits.
reveal: Kawai wore Godzilla for the last of the original run and kept working the creature circuit long after — one of the anonymous professionals who kept the monster walking between its more famous performers.

production: Gamera 2 / Godzilla, Mothra and King Ghidorah
knownFor: The face of the rival franchise — the giant flying turtle Gamera — and later Godzilla's nemesis Ghidorah.
reveal: Ohashi carried Gamera through the acclaimed Heisei trilogy, then crossed the aisle to play King Ghidorah against Godzilla. A stunt coordinator who's since directed, he's worn both sides of Japan's biggest monster rivalry.

knownFor: A stuntwoman who quietly broke a barrier the genre never talks about.
reveal: Kameyama played the winged Super Gyaos — recognized as the first woman ever to perform a full daikaiju, a giant monster, on film. Four decades of men in the suit, and then her.

knownFor: The man inside one of the most beloved — and most frightening — modern Godzilla designs.
reveal: Yoshida wore the malevolent, blank-white-eyed Godzilla of GMK, a fan favorite for its pitiless vengeful stare, and played other Toho creatures besides. The scariest Godzilla in decades was his to carry.

character: Many aliens — Klingon, Cardassian, Vidiian, Kreetassan…
knownFor: Holds the record for the most distinct characters played in the Star Trek franchise — a dozen-plus, most under full makeup.
reveal: He was a different species almost every time. The rare on-camera constant across Klingons, Cardassians, Vidiians and more was that you never once recognised him from role to role — the whole point of a Westmore appliance.

character: K'Ehleyr / the female Q / Dr. Selar
knownFor: Played a half-Klingon, an Andorian, a Vulcan and a member of the Q Continuum across the franchise.
reveal: Four different Trek species, one actor. The Klingon forehead alone was a two-hour build; she wore several of Westmore's most involved appliances and made each read as a distinct person.

knownFor: The towering, near-silent valet to Lwaxana Troi — and, elsewhere, Lurch and Twin Peaks' Giant.
reveal: At seven feet, he's built to loom before any makeup goes on. The trick of his career is how completely the stillness and the appliances erase the warm, articulate actor underneath.

character: Sarek / a Romulan Commander / a Klingon
knownFor: The definitive Vulcan father — and the only TOS actor to play a Vulcan, a Romulan and a Klingon.
reveal: Three of Star Trek's founding alien races wore his face. Spock's father Sarek is the one everyone remembers; the Romulan Commander and the Klingon captain of the films were the same man, differently pointed and ridged.

knownFor: One of the original TOS Klingon commanders, brought back in makeup decades later for DS9 and Voyager.
reveal: He first played Kang smooth-browed in 1968 and returned in the 1990s under a full ridged Westmore forehead — the same character, re-sculpted for a new era of Klingon.

knownFor: Worf's Klingon brother — and, off the shelf, the Candyman.
reveal: Under the ridged brow and mane he's unrecognisable as the velvet-voiced horror icon of the same years. Two designed faces, one actor, running in parallel.

knownFor: The face and hands inside Admiral Ackbar — 'It's a trap!' — for nearly forty years.
reveal: A puppeteer wearing an animatronic head: he worked Ackbar's mouth and eyes from inside the costume while others voiced him. The salmon-coloured commander never had a human face on screen, and this is it.

knownFor: Played the Neimoidian viceroy and a cone-headed Jedi Master in the same trilogy.
reveal: Two very different prequel aliens, one actor under Nick Dudman's appliances — plus a couple of smaller masked parts. The Neimoidian's slow, hooded look is entirely built; so is the Jedi's elongated skull.

production: Rogue One: A Star Wars Story
knownFor: The dry, reprogrammed Imperial droid — and a career of hiding inside robots and voices.
reveal: On set he played the seven-foot droid in a mo-cap rig on stilts; the finished K-2SO is animation over his performance. It's the same trick behind Sonny in I, Robot — the actor is the ghost in the machine.

knownFor: The physical Dark Lord of the prologue — and a stuntman who doubled as dozens of orcs.
reveal: The armoured Sauron who takes the Ring on the battlefield is a real man in a Weta suit, not a digital effect. He also played orcs, a Uruk-hai and other creatures the same shoot — pure hidden-performer work.

knownFor: The pale orc who hunts Thorin's company.
reveal: Performed as motion-capture and finished as a CG orc, so the towering albino Gundabad villain is his movement and his eyeline — the face is built over him frame by frame.

knownFor: The silent, grinning clown of the Terrifier films.
reveal: Everything unsettling about Art — the black-and-white grease, the pinhead smile — is Damien Leone's makeup worn by a trained, expressive mime. The performer is warm and chatty; the mask never breaks.

knownFor: The rebooted Jason — widely rated one of the most physical takes on the character.
reveal: Under the hockey mask and the KNB prosthetic damage is a genial giant of an actor. The whole role is body language read through a mask that never shows a human expression.

character: Jason Voorhees (the boy in the lake)
knownFor: The very first Jason — the child who lunges from the water in the final scare.
reveal: Before the hockey mask and the decades of sequels, Jason was a drowned boy in Tom Savini's makeup, played for a single shock cut. That brief, deformed face launched a forty-year franchise.

knownFor: Rob Zombie's towering Michael Myers.
reveal: A former pro wrestler under a bleached mask, giving the Shape a sheer physical mass. You never see his face and never hear him — the entire performance is size and stillness inside a boilersuit.

knownFor: The remake-era Leatherface, behind a mask literally made of skin.
reveal: The horror of Leatherface is a mask worn over a mask — KNB's 'human skin' appliance over the actor's own face. What's left to act with is posture and a chainsaw.

character: The voice of the Daleks & Cybermen
knownFor: The modern Dalek 'EX-TER-MIN-ATE' — and the Cybermen, and the Judoon, and more.
reveal: Every menace that grates or booms out of a Doctor Who prop is one man with a ring modulator. He performs them live on set into the machine, so the actors have something to flinch at.

character: The Ood, Judoon and many monsters
knownFor: The go-to monster performer of the revived series.
reveal: If a rubber-and-silicone creature shuffles through modern Who, odds are it's him inside it — the Ood's tentacled face, the rhino-headed Judoon and dozens more, each a Millennium FX build worn by the same quiet actor.

knownFor: The definitive classic-series Dalek operator, hidden inside the shell for over twenty years.
reveal: A Dalek is two performers — a voice, and a person folded inside working the arms, eyestalk and skirt with their knees and shoulders. For decades that person was him. Nobody ever saw him do it.

character: The Muppet Show workshop — Kermit, Rowlf, Waldorf
knownFor: Kermit the Frog was his own left hand and voice for thirty-five years.
reveal: The most recognisable puppeteer in the world was, on screen, never visible at all — just a green frog on his arm. The gentleness everyone felt from Kermit was him, working below the frame.

knownFor: The weirdo daredevil and the oblivious scientist — one performer, decades deep.
reveal: He started as a puppet builder and stayed on as one of the core hands. Gonzo's whole melancholy-brave interior life comes from a man you'd walk right past, working an arm above his head.

character: Count von Count, Floyd Pepper & Robin
knownFor: The Count's 'One! Ah-ah-ah!' and half the Electric Mayhem.
reveal: A vast range of characters — a counting vampire, a laid-back bassist, a small nephew frog — all one performer's hands and voice, none of it ever wearing his face.

knownFor: Turned a spare red puppet into the most famous monster on Sesame Street.
reveal: The falsetto giggle that a generation of toddlers loves came from a tall, deep-voiced man working the puppet from below. Elmo has a face known worldwide; his performer's is not.

knownFor: Carried Kermit and Ernie for decades after their creator.
reveal: He inherited the most famous frog in the world and kept him alive for a quarter-century — an act of total self-erasure, since the job is to be a voice and a hand and never, ever the face.

portrait.author: Chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff

character: Pepe the King Prawn, Rowlf & Bobo
knownFor: The scheming prawn 'okay?' and the modern Rowlf.
reveal: One of the current lead performers, holding several signature characters at once — a Spanish-accented prawn, a piano-playing dog, a sweet dim bear — while remaining, on camera, nobody.

knownFor: One of the suit actors who carried Godzilla through the modern Toho era.
reveal: Forty-plus kilos of foam-latex monster, filmed over a miniature Tokyo in stage heat — the roar and the stomp are effects, but the weight and the walk are a man sealed inside the suit.

knownFor: A master of classical kyōgen theatre who moved Shin Godzilla.
reveal: Toho's reboot dropped the rubber suit for motion capture — and hired a centuries-tradition stage actor to give the creature its stiff, agonised gait. The most modern Godzilla is driven by the oldest kind of Japanese performance.

production: Planet of the Apes trilogy & many
knownFor: The movement coach and performer behind whole populations of digital apes.
reveal: He teaches the cast to move like apes, then vanishes into the herd himself as mo-cap. When a blockbuster is full of creatures that move like one organism, it's often because he's underneath the choreography and inside it.

knownFor: Caesar's gentle, wise orang confidant across three films.
reveal: A human actor, in a mo-cap suit, playing an orangutan so tenderly that audiences forget there was ever a person — the whole performance survives translation into fur and a wholly different face.

production: War for the Planet of the Apes
knownFor: The skittish, funny zoo escapee of the third film.
reveal: A recognisable comic actor disappearing completely into a nervy chimpanzee — the timing is his, the face is entirely built over his capture data.

character: The Brain, Kif Kroker, Egon Spengler
knownFor: The Orson-Welles rasp behind a world-conquering lab mouse.
reveal: Try-to-take-over-the-world Brain, the put-upon Kif, and a note-perfect Welles for archival gigs — all one throat you've never seen but have heard for decades.

knownFor: Sang the nations of the world in one breath as Yakko.
reveal: A zany elder Warner, a giddy lab mouse and a turtle in a half-shell — hundreds of episodes, one unseen performer whose actual face means nothing to the millions who know the voices.

character: Winnie the Pooh, Tigger, Darkwing Duck
knownFor: Inherited Pooh and Tigger and made them permanently his.
reveal: A generation's bedtime bear is his voice; so is the bouncing tiger and a caped crime-fighting duck. The comfort those characters give is entirely a man you'd never recognise.

production: Star Wars: The Clone Wars & many
knownFor: Voiced an entire army of identical clones — every one, in conversation with itself.
reveal: He plays whole battalions who are all technically the same man, plus most of animation's non-verbal animals and monsters. An unseen actor who is, some scenes, the entire speaking cast.

character: Bender, Jake the Dog, Marcus Fenix
production: Futurama / Adventure Time / Gears of War
knownFor: The gravel behind a beer-drinking robot and a shape-shifting dog.
reveal: A cynical bending unit, a stretchy magic dog and a chainsaw-gun soldier — one big warm voice doing all of it, recognisable instantly and yet a face nobody pictures.

portrait.author: Super Festivals from Ft. Lauderdale, USA

knownFor: Built the beeps and whirs that read as a whole personality.
reveal: Neither droid has a mouth or a single word, yet both are beloved characters — because a sound designer performed them out of synthesisers and his own voice. The 'acting' is entirely his, with no face and no lines at all.

knownFor: Won the Best Actor Oscar for a role half of which is buried under simian makeup.
reveal: The transformation was shot with coloured filters and coloured makeup so Hyde 'grew' on camera in one take. The Academy honoured a performance you can barely recognise as the handsome leading man underneath.

knownFor: The definitive sound-era Hunchback, under a hump, a fused eye and rubber flesh.
reveal: He wore a forty-pound harness and full facial appliances in California summer heat, and reportedly stayed in the makeup between setups. The tenderness that survives all that latex is the whole performance.

knownFor: A teenager with craniodiaphyseal dysplasia, built entirely in prosthetics.
reveal: Westmore won an Oscar for the makeup; Stoltz acted a whole warm, funny kid through hours of appliance every day. Audiences forget there's a conventionally handsome actor under the reconstructed face.

knownFor: The lightning-streaked hair and the hiss — an icon built from a few minutes of screen time.
reveal: She's on screen as the Bride for only a moment, yet the Nefertiti neck, the scars and the electrified bouffant made her immortal. In the same film she plays Mary Shelley, unmade-up — most viewers never connect the two.

production: House of Frankenstein / Abbott and Costello Meet Frankenstein
knownFor: The Universal Monster of the later cycle — the flat head everyone pictures.
reveal: A Western heavy who inherited the bolts and the brow from Karloff and wore them longest across the studio's monster rally pictures. Under the greenish flat-top is a lanky cowboy actor.

knownFor: The Creature's graceful underwater swimming — a whole second performer for one monster.
reveal: The Gill-man was two people: one on land, and this competitive swimmer sealed in the foam suit for the underwater ballet, holding his breath between air hoses. The eerie glide that sells the creature is entirely him.

knownFor: Hammer's towering Dracula — and, before that, their bandaged Mummy and stitched Creature.
reveal: Everyone remembers the fangs, but his first monsters were fully built: Hammer's scarred Frankenstein Creature and the wrapped, shuffling Mummy hid the elegant actor completely. Six-foot-five of him, disappeared under appliances.

knownFor: The first actor to play both Banner and a fully performance-captured Hulk.
reveal: Earlier Hulks were animation over a stunt reference; here the giant's face is mapped from his own, so Banner and the Hulk finally share a performance. Nine feet of green rage carrying a recognisable human's eyes.

knownFor: The blue Na'vi lead of the highest-grossing films ever made.
reveal: Filmed in a mo-cap volume with a head-rig camera an inch from his face, then rendered ten feet tall and blue. The whole saga rests on a performance the audience only ever sees translated into another species.

knownFor: A seven-foot creature specialist inside dozens of monsters.
reveal: Predators, the hound Sammael, werewolves, the Colossus — if a tall practical creature stalks through a 2000s film, odds are he's the one sweating inside it. A career built on never being seen.

knownFor: 'I have spoken.' — the gravel of a moisture-farming Ugnaught.
reveal: The weathered, wise little alien is a Legacy Effects puppet on set and this unmistakable rasp in the booth. You'd never place the famously craggy movie star inside the small tusked creature.

knownFor: The wounded, wisecracking raccoon at the heart of the Guardians.
reveal: A leading man you'd recognise instantly on a red carpet, entirely unrecognisable as the CG rodent — no makeup, no capture, just the voice doing all the disappearing.

knownFor: Played Rocket on set so the cast had someone real to act with — then played Kraglin too.
reveal: He crawls through every scene as the raccoon's stand-in and eyeline, hunched on knee-pads, before another voice and a render replace him. His actual credited role, Kraglin, is the only time you see his face.

knownFor: Three words — 'I am Groot' — carrying a whole emotional range.
reveal: An action star reduced to one repeated line and a tree's silhouette, and somehow it reads as tenderness, fear and sacrifice. The face is bark; the acting is all in the voice.

production: The Texas Chainsaw Massacre 2 / The Devil's Rejects
knownFor: The metal-plated head of Chop Top and the leering Otis.
reveal: Chop Top's exposed steel plate and wig, scratched at with a heated coat-hanger, turn a soft-spoken actor into something feral. The design does half the work; he does the rest.

production: House of 1000 Corpses / The Devil's Rejects
knownFor: The foul-mouthed killer clown of Rob Zombie's films.
reveal: A prolific character heavy finally made an icon by a full clown makeup — greasepaint, rotten teeth and a filthy grin doing the transformation the scripts only imply.

portrait.author: Gage Skidmore from Surprise, AZ, United States of America

knownFor: 'D'oh!' — plus Krusty, Barney, Grampa and half of Springfield.
reveal: The most recognisable voice in television history belongs to a mild, unassuming actor most fans couldn't pick out of a crowd. Homer alone is decades of one man erasing himself into a yellow cartoon.

knownFor: The gravel-and-honey murmur under the blue tower of hair.
reveal: The voice is so specific it seems impossible it's a real person's — but it's her own, roughened, held for thirty-plus years. Marge and her chain-smoking sisters are all one unseen actress.

knownFor: 'Excellent.' — the wheeze of Springfield's villain and its cheeriest neighbour.
reveal: A satirist and musician voicing a dozen opposite characters in the same breath. The face behind Burns, Flanders, Smithers and Skinner is one you've simply never had reason to learn.

knownFor: The laugh — and the Ice King, and the Mayor of Townsville.
reveal: An entire generation's most familiar voice is a man on a mic whose face means nothing to them. The absorbent optimist, and half of animation besides, all one throat.

knownFor: The calm blade of Jack and the bureaucratic Hermes.
reveal: A vast animation range — a wandering samurai, a Jamaican civil servant, Green Lantern — carried by an actor most know only by ear.

knownFor: A bass rumble that has anchored gargoyles, aliens and a hellspawn.
reveal: That unmistakable voice gives weight to characters with no human face at all — a stone gargoyle, an alien warrior, an animated antihero — while the actor himself stays offscreen.

knownFor: A baby and the toughest Powerpuff, both in the same career.
reveal: A singer and actress who became, unseen, a toddler adventurer and a scrappy superhero to millions of kids who'd never recognise her.

knownFor: One of the most-cast voices in animation — a mystery-solver and a fire-nation princess.
reveal: Heroines and terrifying villains from the same booth, hundreds of them, by a performer whose face is entirely beside the point of the work.

knownFor: Cartman, Stan, Mr. Garrison and most of the town.
reveal: Half a foul-mouthed town is two men doing pitched-up voices into cheap mics. The face behind Cartman is a filmmaker you'd never connect to that shrill third-grade snarl.

knownFor: For a decade, the most-cast voice in video games.
reveal: A wisecracking treasure hunter, the Penguin, Deadpool, half a console generation of NPCs — all one working actor you'd never spot at the coffee shop.

knownFor: A smuggler father, the Clown Prince, Booker DeWitt.
reveal: He carries whole prestige games on a voice and a capture rig, disappearing into men who look nothing like him — the acting survives entirely into another face.

portrait.author:
Troy_baker_taiyoucon_2011.jpg: Kevin Dooley from Chander, AZ, USA
derivative work: Masem (talk)

knownFor: That gravel is in more credits than almost anyone alive.
reveal: A laconic bounty hunter, a clawed mutant, and thousands of grunts and villains besides — a career measured in roles nobody attaches to a face.

knownFor: Long called the most prolific female voice in games.
reveal: Players who spent a hundred hours as her Shepard would pass her on the street without a flicker of recognition — the whole performance lives in the headset.

knownFor: The cackling pun-machine host of the crypt.
reveal: The ghoul was a Kevin Yagher puppet; the manic laugh and every groan-worthy pun were this actor, unseen, giving a pile of animatronic bones a whole personality.

character: The Hulk & a bestiary of monsters
knownFor: The go-to roar for large green rage and countless beasts.
reveal: If a game monster bellows, odds are it's him — a warm, ordinary-looking actor who has spent a career being things that aren't human at all.

knownFor: The screech of a treacherous jet robot and a whole barnyard.
reveal: A Decepticon's whine and both a cow and a chicken from the same throat — decades of characters, zero of them his own face.

production: Star Wars: The Clone Wars / Disney
knownFor: A Sith Lord, a chipmunk, Brain from Inspector Gadget.
reveal: An uncanny range from menace to the cartoonish, carried by a performer the audience only ever meets by ear.

knownFor: Inherited Yoda for a decade of animated Star Wars.
reveal: He voiced the little green Jedi Master and the fish-headed admiral and the narrator besides — three unmistakable characters, one unseen man.

knownFor: The middle Warner sibling and the spinning marsupial.
reveal: A Liverpudlian gulp of a cartoon voice and a video-game mascot's yelps, from a session singer you've heard far more than seen.

knownFor: A whole cartoon range, plus a bench of Springfield women.
reveal: The cute youngest Warner, the modern Daffy, Dot's every wink — hundreds of characters by a performer whose own face means nothing to the audience.

knownFor: A deep, elastic voice under villains beyond counting.
reveal: That booming laugh has been the Joker, the Shredder, Cleveland Brown — a huge unseen range from one of animation's busiest actors.

production: Codename: Kids Next Door / Tiny Toons
knownFor: A generation of cartoon girls, cool and manic alike.
reveal: An actress and musician who became, unseen, a spy kid, a hyperactive animal-lover and a next-door neighbour to millions who never learned her face.

knownFor: A dim dad, a diabolical baby and a talking dog — same throat.
reveal: Three of the four leads of his own show are him, pitched and bent past recognition; the man behind the whole household hides in plain sound.

knownFor: Half a foul-mouthed town, including a boy who only mumbles.
reveal: He voices a whole side of South Park, including one character muffled beyond words entirely — the ultimate hidden performance, a face inside a parka inside a cartoon.

knownFor: A blue blur and a Renaissance assassin.
reveal: Two mascots for two industries, both his voice, neither his face — a working actor most know only as characters.

knownFor: Carried Porky's stutter forward after Mel Blanc.
reveal: He studied one legend's throat for years to inherit a stuttering pig, then voiced a young Jedi in the toys besides — an unseen custodian of famous voices.

knownFor: 'It's a trap!' — six words that outlived the film.
reveal: A radio-drama veteran who gave the fish-headed admiral one immortal line; the puppet was others', the voice that became a meme was his.

knownFor: 'EX-TER-MIN-ATE' for a generation of classic Who.
reveal: He ring-modulated the Daleks and Cybermen for decades, then went home and voiced a zip-mouthed puppet on a children's show — two of Britain's most familiar voices, one unseen man.

knownFor: Invented the Dalek voice, and Captain Pugwash besides.
reveal: The original grating snarl of the Daleks was his, worked out with a ring modulator in 1963; he also voiced Bill and Ben and a cartoon pirate captain — a founding hidden voice of British TV.

knownFor: The first woman to become a lead Muppet performer.
reveal: For four decades a small orange monster and a prim pianist were her hands and voice, while she — a working stage and screen actress — stayed entirely out of frame.

knownFor: The shaggy mammoth friend and the worried Telly — and Audrey II on Broadway.
reveal: He is the front half of a giant walking rug and the anxious purple monster, and off Sesame Street he puppeteered a man-eating plant; a lifetime spent inside characters no audience ever sees him in.

knownFor: The towering Jason of the crossover.
reveal: A stunt coordinator who put on the hockey mask himself — seven feet of silent menace built from posture, since the face never shows a thing.

portrait.author: Super Festivals from Ft. Lauderdale, USA

knownFor: The floating, grinning Gentlemen of 'Hush'.
reveal: He wore Optic Nerve's most nightmarish Buffy appliances — the silent Gentlemen, an übervamp, Gnarl — a specialist in vanishing under the show's scariest builds.

knownFor: Lando's cackling co-pilot at the Battle of Endor.
reveal: A puppeteer inside the cockpit working the wrinkled Sullustan's face — one of a troupe of hidden hands who made Jabba's palace and the Rebel fleet feel alive.

production: Rogue One: A Star Wars Story
knownFor: The body inside the most famous helmet in film.
reveal: A former boxer who physically played Vader — the mask, the cape, the looming walk — while another voice finished him. Elsewhere he's been a dozen Doctor Who and fantasy creatures, always unseen.

knownFor: Cocteau's tragic, magnificent Beast.
reveal: Hours in a fur-and-fang makeup so heavy it reportedly took five hours to apply, worn by a matinee idol — the audience aches for a face they never actually see until the spell breaks.

portrait.author:
original: Carl Van Vechten
derivative work: …trialsanderrors

knownFor: The permanent rictus smile that later inspired the Joker.
reveal: Dental prosthetics and hooks held his mouth in a frozen grin for the whole film. The anguish he plays through that fixed smile is entirely the eyes — a silent-era master acting around a face he couldn't move.

knownFor: The broken-necked, leering blacksmith — his own favorite role.
reveal: Jack Pierce buried the smooth matinee Dracula under a snarl of beard, a twisted spine and a hangman's-scar neck. It's the performance where Lugosi vanishes most completely into someone else.

knownFor: The dreamy artist Fraggle — and half of the Gelfling love story.
reveal: She puppeteered a whole gentle Fraggle and, in a foam-and-cable suit on The Dark Crystal, was the Gelfling Kira from the inside. A career spent being creatures, never seen as herself.

knownFor: The brash, athletic Fraggle in the swimming hole.
reveal: The bounding energy of Red is one performer's arm and voice under the rock — later a Pixar animator, but here entirely hidden inside a puppet a generation grew up with.

portrait.author: Kris Awesome from San Diego, CA, USA

character: Contortion creatures — the Rag Man & others
knownFor: A contortionist who folds himself into things that move wrong.
reveal: No CGI — his own double-jointed body, sometimes under appliances, becomes the crawling, backwards-bending horror. The unsettling motion is a real man in the room, not a render.

knownFor: Was Donald's furious quack for over fifty years.
reveal: One man made a duck's unintelligible rage beloved for half a century — a voice so specific Disney had him dub Donald into other languages himself, because no one else could do it.

portrait.author: Alan Light; cropped by Beyond My Ken (talk) 03:00, 27 July 2015 (UTC)

knownFor: The original Goofy 'a-hyuck' — and a real circus clown.
reveal: A former newspaper cartoonist and clown who gave Goofy his guffaw and Pluto his pants-less bark; the voice under the Disney everyman is a face nobody pictures.

knownFor: 'Boop-oop-a-doop' and Popeye's beanpole sweetheart.
reveal: Two of animation's most distinct female voices, decades apart, from one performer the audience only ever met by ear.

character: Boris Badenov & the Haunted Mansion Ghost Host
knownFor: Another 'Man of a Thousand Voices' — a spy, a Beatle, a doughboy, a ghost.
reveal: His voice welcomes every rider into the Haunted Mansion and schemed as Boris; he dubbed and narrated so much of mid-century media that you've heard him constantly and never once seen him.

knownFor: Half the Hanna-Barbera bench in one throat.
reveal: A pic-a-nic-basket bear, a blue hound, a pink lion 'Heavens to Murgatroyd' — the voice teacher who quietly populated Saturday mornings, unseen.

knownFor: The 'Ruh-roh' of a cowardly Great Dane.
reveal: Scooby, Astro, Boo-Boo, Bamm-Bamm, Papa Smurf — an enormous hidden range, most of it wordless dog-talk and mutters, from one unseen actor.

character: Tony the Tiger & the Grinch's song
production: Kellogg's / How the Grinch Stole Christmas
knownFor: 'They're grrreat!' — and the booming 'You're a Mean One, Mr. Grinch.'
reveal: That cavernous bass sold cereal for fifty years and sang the Grinch's roast uncredited; a voice everyone knows attached to a face almost no one does.

character: Winnie the Pooh, the Cheshire Cat & Kaa
knownFor: The original, wispy voice of Pooh Bear.
reveal: A gentle, unmistakable rasp that was a silly old bear, a grinning cat and a hypnotic snake — a character actor far better known by ear than by face.

knownFor: 'Poor Unfortunate Souls' — the definitive Disney villainess.
reveal: A veteran comic actress poured relish and menace into a purple cecaelia; the tentacled villain is one of animation's great performances, and few could pick the performer out of a room.

knownFor: The blustering, near-blind old man.
reveal: Better known on camera as Gilligan's millionaire, but his voice is the one that made a squinting curmudgeon a household name — the character seen everywhere, the man behind it rarely connected to it.

knownFor: The lasagna-loving cat's deadpan drawl.
reveal: That laconic voice was a fat orange cat and a cartoon Bill Murray both — an unseen actor whose delivery defined characters people quote without knowing his name.

portrait.author: Published on zenithcity.com by David Ouse

knownFor: 'Yabba-dabba-doo!'
reveal: The bellow of prime-time animation's first leading man was a working radio and character actor — the caveman is instantly known; the voice actor, not at all.

knownFor: The patient wife of Bedrock and the Jetsons' sardonic maid.
reveal: Two eras of Hanna-Barbera women from one performer, entirely offscreen — a hidden mainstay of the studio's golden age.

knownFor: 'Zoinks!' — the perpetually hungry beatnik.
reveal: America's Top 40 host by day, but to a generation of kids he was a cowardly cartoon teenager and nothing else — a famous voice whose two identities rarely met.

knownFor: Voiced something like five thousand trailers.
reveal: The most heard voice in Hollywood belonged to a man audiences never saw or named; for decades, the epic hush before every blockbuster was one unseen performer setting the scene.
